refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path during simulated and live operational scenarios. Document network conditions and acceptance evidence.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement packet loss detection and reporting mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall employ error correction or redundancy strategies to minimize packet loss impact on command and feedback streams.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert operators when packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission with acknowledgment-based flow control in the teleoperation communication stack.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual network paths or multi-carrier) with automatic failover to maintain packet delivery below threshold.
DP-C: Integrate network quality monitoring with dynamic codec/compression adjustment and operator alerting when packet loss approaches threshold.
DP-D: Combine selective retransmission for critical commands, FEC for telemetry streams, and real-time packet loss monitoring with operator feedback.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Combine selective retransmission for critical commands (e.g., emergency stop, mode changes), forward error correction for continuous telemetry streams, and real-time packet loss monitoring with operator alerting when packet loss approaches the customer-defined threshold.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation communication logic (retransmission, FEC) with operator interface (alerting), but this is a necessary and acceptable coupling because operator awareness of communication quality is integral to safe teleoperation.
DP-D also couples with network infrastructure (link quality), but this is managed through the Network Link interface and does not create circular dependencies.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is decoupled from specific hardware implementations (radio, cellular, wired) and allows flexibility in network selection. The design is modular: retransmission logic, FEC codec, and monitoring can be implemented independently and composed. No evidence of axiomatic design violation (independence axiom).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable given the agreed network conditions and available bandwidth.
Assumption: Selective retransmission does not introduce unacceptable latency for critical commands.
Risk: FEC overhead may consume bandwidth; if network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may be counterproductive.
Risk: Operator alerting mechanism must be designed to avoid alert fatigue; excessive warnings may reduce operator trust or situational awareness.
Risk: Retransmission strategy must account for command ordering and state consistency in remote equipment control.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) which command types trigger selective retransmission vs. fire-and-forget, (2) FEC code rate and overhead budget, (3) packet loss threshold for operator alerting (e.g., 0.5% as warning, 1% as critical), and (4) maximum acceptable latency for retransmitted critical commands. This will reduce ambiguity and support detailed design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication quality assurance for remote equipment operation, including packet loss detection, error correction, retransmission logic, and operator alerting. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (for command execution and telemetry generation), Safety Services (for safety-critical command validation and alerts), and the Network Link (for physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, mode changes, emergency stop) from the tele-operator; Teleoperation module applies retransmission and validation logic before forwarding to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and packet loss metrics to the tele-operator; Teleoperation module applies FEC and monitors delivery quality.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and critical command validation from Safety Services; sends packet loss alerts and communication quality warnings to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with underlying network infrastructure (radio, cellular, wired) for packet transmission and reception; monitors link quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss).

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own packet loss management. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple subsystems and obscure ownership of communication quality logic.

Vehicle module owns equipment control and telemetry generation but should not own communication quality assurance. Mixing vehicle dynamics with network error correction violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling between mechanical/hydraulic systems and communication protocols.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of packet loss management, error correction, and operator alerting. All logic for maintaining communication quality (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) is cohesive and directly supports the teleoperation use case. Minimal diffusion; clear ownership boundary.

Safety Services owns safety validation and critical alerts but should not own communication protocol logic. However, Safety Services must receive packet loss alerts from Teleoperation and may enforce operational constraints (e.g., reduce speed if packet loss exceeds threshold). This is a consumer relationship, not ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of packet loss management because: (1) it is functionally cohesive—all communication quality assurance logic is directly related to remote operation, (2) it has clear external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) that define its boundaries, (3) it minimizes diffusion by centralizing error correction and monitoring logic, and (4) it allows Vehicle and Safety Services to remain focused on their respective concerns (equipment control and safety validation).
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must process all commands and telemetry. Mitigation: Design with asynchronous, non-blocking communication patterns and consider multi-threaded or event-driven architecture.

Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Network Link if network-specific details (e.g., radio protocol, cellular carrier) leak into the module. Mitigation: Abstract network interface behind a clean API; allow pluggable network implementations.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may create circular dependencies if Safety Services needs to validate commands before Teleoperation applies retransmission. Mitigation: Clarify the order of operations: Teleoperation applies retransmission, then Safety Services validates; or Safety Services pre-validates, then Teleoperation applies retransmission. Document the contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Teleoperation module's internal architecture: (1) separate the communication quality layer (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) from the command/telemetry marshaling layer, (2) specify the API contract with Vehicle (how commands are delivered, how telemetry is received), (3) define the SafetyAlert handshake (when and how packet loss alerts are raised), and (4) document the Network Link abstraction (what network metrics are available, how failover is triggered). This will reduce integration risk and clarify ownership boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D directly addresses the refined FR by implementing packet loss detection, error correction (FEC), retransmission for critical commands, and operator alerting. The DP is specific enough to guide detailed design (e.g., FEC code selection, retransmission timeout tuning) while remaining flexible to network conditions. No independence axiom violation detected; the DP does not create circular dependencies or over-constrain the design space.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is a natural fit for DP-D. All communication quality logic is cohesive and localized.

Moderate interface risk: SafetyAlert interface requires careful design to avoid circular dependencies between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Recommend explicit sequencing: Teleoperation detects packet loss, raises SafetyAlert, Safety Services decides on operational response (e.g., reduce speed, alert operator).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. The module boundary is clear: it owns all logic for maintaining communication quality (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) and interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (commands/telemetry), Safety Services (alerts), and Network Link (physical transmission). No evidence of diffusion or ownership ambiguity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end during integration testing and field trials. Acceptance criteria (≤1% under agreed network conditions) are objective and testable. Recommend establishing a test harness that simulates network conditions (latency, jitter, packet loss) and validates that the Teleoperation module maintains the target threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss management; Vehicle module owns equipment control; Safety Services owns safety validation. Interfaces are well-defined (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No ambiguity in responsibility or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC code rate, retransmission timeout, and packet loss alerting thresholds. This will reduce ambiguity in detailed design.
Document the SafetyAlert handshake between Teleoperation and Safety Services to clarify the order of operations and avoid circular dependencies.
Define the Network Link abstraction to specify what network metrics are available (latency, jitter, packet loss) and how failover is triggered.

Establish acceptance test criteria: measure packet loss under representative network conditions (e.g., 4G LTE, satellite, private radio) and validate that the Teleoperation module maintains the customer-defined threshold.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed and addresses a critical safety and quality-of-service requirement. DP-D is a sound, decoupled design that avoids independence axiom violations. Teleoperation module is the optimal owner with high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All gates pass. However, DP-D is not yet atomic—it comprises three distinct strategies (selective retransmission, FEC, monitoring with alerting) that should be decomposed into sub-FRs and sub-DPs to guide detailed design and reduce implementation risk. Recommend decomposing to the next level to specify FEC code selection, retransmission logic, and alerting thresholds.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The Teleoperation module shall detect and report packet loss in real-time, providing metrics to the operator and Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The Teleoperation module shall apply forward error correction to telemetry streams to reduce the impact of packet loss on sensor data fidelity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The Teleoperation module shall implement selective retransmission for critical commands (e.g., emergency stop, mode changes) to ensure reliable delivery.

FR-NGHE-009.4: The Teleoperation module shall alert the operator and Safety Services when packet loss approaches or exceeds the customer-defined threshold, enabling graceful degradation or emergency response.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable given agreed network conditions and available bandwidth.
Selective retransmission does not introduce unacceptable latency for critical commands (assume max latency budget is defined in system requirements).
Operator alerting mechanism can be designed to avoid alert fatigue and maintain operator trust.
Network Link interface provides sufficient metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) to enable adaptive communication quality management.
Safety Services will enforce operational constraints (e.g., reduce speed) in response to packet loss alerts from Teleoperation.
open_risks: FEC overhead may consume bandwidth; if network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may be counterproductive. Recommend bandwidth budget analysis in detailed design.
Retransmission strategy must account for command ordering and state consistency in remote equipment control. Recommend formal specification of command semantics (idempotent vs. stateful).
SafetyAlert interface may create circular dependencies if not carefully designed. Recommend explicit sequencing and contract definition.
Network failover logic (e.g., switching from primary to backup link) must be transparent to Teleoperation module to avoid coupling. Recommend abstraction via Network Link interface.
Packet loss measurement methodology must be agreed with customer to ensure acceptance criteria are met. Recommend early alignment on test conditions and acceptance evidence.
evidence_needed: Network bandwidth budget and FEC overhead analysis to validate that FEC is feasible without exceeding available bandwidth.
Command latency budget and retransmission timeout analysis to ensure critical commands are delivered within acceptable latency.
Operator alerting design and human factors analysis to validate that alerting thresholds and mechanisms do not reduce operator situational awareness.
SafetyAlert interface specification and formal verification to ensure no circular dependencies between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Network failover test plan and acceptance criteria to validate that packet loss remains below threshold during link transitions.
Field trial data from representative network conditions (4G LTE, satellite, private radio) to validate that the Teleoperation module meets customer-defined packet loss threshold.
